main_summary.summary: Dr. H. Jack Geiger, interviewed in Brooklyn on March 16, 2013 by John Dittmer, traces his trajectory from an Upper West Side adolescence (early 1940s), through years living in Canada Lee's Harlem apartment, into civil rights organizing at the University of Wisconsin and University of Chicago (Congress of Racial Equality, American Veterans Committee), service as a merchant marine radio officer aboard the SS Booker T. Washington under Black captain Hugh Mulzac, medical training at Western Reserve, and a 1958 fellowship in apartheid South Africa with Sidney and Emily Kark at the Pholela rural health center and the Lamontville Zulu housing project. Geiger details the 1964 founding of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, his August 1964 Freedom Summer field-coordinator role in Jackson, the December 1964 Delta Ministry meeting in Greenville that birthed the community-health-center proposal, and his partnership with Dr. Count D. Gibson Jr at Tufts. He recounts negotiating with R. Sargent Shriver Jr and Dr. Julius B. Richmond at OEO, securing veto-proof funding by routing the grant through Tufts, choosing Mound Bayou in Bolivar County, Mississippi, and building with Dr. John W. Hatch, Andrew B. James, and L.C. Dorsey what became the Tufts-Delta Health Center, the North Bolivar County Farm Cooperative, and the federal community-health-center system that now operates at over 8,000 sites.
key_themes: community health center origins and OEO War on Poverty
Medical Committee for Human Rights and Freedom Summer
social determinants of health (Virchow, Sidney Kark's Pholela model)
Mound Bayou and Bolivar County, Mississippi as Smithsonian-grade institutional history
civil rights organizing across SNCC, CORE, SCLC, NAACP, MFDP and the Delta Ministry
main_summary.historical_significance:
Geiger is one of the founders of the U.S. community health center movement and a co-organizer of the Medical Committee for Human Rights. This interview is a primary source for the December 1964 Greenville meeting at which the community-health-center concept first translated into a concrete Tufts-sponsored project; for the OEO negotiations with R. Sargent Shriver Jr and Dr. Julius B. Richmond that produced the office of health affairs; and for the Tufts-Delta Health Center at Mound Bayou as the first rural U.S. application of the South African Pholela model.

Geiger also documents from inside the room civil-rights-medicine intersections rarely captured elsewhere: the 1942 first threatened March on Washington that produced FDR's defense-plant non-discrimination executive order; the December 1947 University of Chicago strike against hospital and medical-school racial discrimination; the Selma 1965 strategy meeting after Bloody Sunday at which he listened to Dr. King, James Forman, and Hosea Williams debate the injunction; and his role transporting John Lewis and Ivanhoe Donaldson to Boston for skull-fracture treatment.

chapters.title: Upper West Side Childhood and Canada Lee
chapters.summary: Geiger's New York City childhood, skipping grades through public school, the magnet high school Townsend Harris, graduating at 14. The Rapp-Coudert Act firings of his teachers. A 1940 backstage visit to Canada Lee after seeing Native Son leads to a years-long second home in Lee's Sugar Hill penthouse at 155th and Edgecombe, where Geiger meets Paul Robeson, Langston Hughes, William Saroyan, and Adam Clayton Powell Jr.

chapters.title: Wisconsin, the March on Washington, CORE
chapters.summary: Admission to the University of Wisconsin at age 15 in September 1941. Pearl Harbor follows months later. Geiger receives a January-February 1942 letter from Bayard Rustin recruiting campus organizers for the first threatened March on Washington that A. Philip Randolph organized to protest defense-plant discrimination; FDR's resulting executive order bars racial discrimination in defense-plant hiring. Geiger meets James Farmer in Chicago in early 1943 and helps start one of the earliest CORE chapters.
related_events: FDR Executive Order 8802 on defense-plant hiring

chapters.title: Merchant Marine and SS Booker T. Washington
chapters.summary: Geiger enlists in the merchant marine in 1943 because it is the only desegregated branch (the National Maritime Union had won the fight to integrate ships). After radio-radar officer school on Gallops Island, he serves on the SS Horseshoe in the Pacific (Ulithi, Leyte Gulf) before joining the SS Booker T. Washington under Hugh Mulzac, the only Black captain in the American fleet, with an integrated officer crew. Repeated dry-docking in segregated Norfolk produces weekly integrate-the-white-waiting-room confrontations.

chapters.title: Chicago Hospital Strike of 1947
chapters.summary: Enrolling pre-med at the University of Chicago in 1946, Geiger works overnight at International News Service and chairs civil-liberties work for the American Veterans Committee. A documented campaign against the University of Chicago hospitals' refusal to admit Black patients culminates in a December 7, 1947 student-faculty strike, with Jeremiah Stamler arriving from Northwestern with a sound truck. The breakthrough comes from following the development office's foundation-visit schedule.

chapters.title: Journalism Career and Western Reserve
chapters.summary: After being warned by Dr. W. Montague Cobb at Howard that AMA blacklisting will prevent admission to any standard medical school, Geiger pivots to science journalism at International News Service. By 1953 he assigns himself to the Association of American Medical Colleges meeting, where Jack McCoy of Western Reserve recruits him into the new integrated curriculum's class of bent-arrow nontraditional admissions.

chapters.title: Discovering Social Medicine
chapters.summary: On the steps of the medical school in his second year, Geiger experiences what he later recognizes as the rediscovery of social medicine, traceable to Virchow and John Simon in 19th-century Europe. Through Margaret Mead and Barry Commoner he reaches Warren Weaver at the Rockefeller Foundation; rather than join Weaver's staff, Geiger learns from him about Dr. John B. Grant's funding of Sidney and Emily Kark in apartheid South Africa.

chapters.title: Pholela and Lamontville
chapters.summary: Geiger spends four and a half months of 1958 in South Africa at Sidney and Emily Kark's Pholela rural health center in Natal province (halfway between Durban and Pietermaritzburg) and at the Lamontville Zulu public housing project near Durban. He observes the pre-Verwoerd window when more than 30 community health centers operated in South Africa, learns the model of community health workers and community-oriented primary care, and reads Sidney Kark's Social Pathology of Syphilis on the political economy of male labor migration.
related_events: creation of South African community health centers under the wartime national health service window

chapters.title: Freedom Summer 1964 in Jackson
chapters.summary: Charles Hudson recruits Geiger to be the August 1964 Medical Committee for Human Rights field coordinator in Jackson at 12 and a half North Farish Street. MCHR provides medical presence for SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and the COFO umbrella, with Tom Levin and others rotating through. Geiger meets Aaron Henry, recruits Dr. Count D. Gibson Jr from Tufts, and develops the COFO creep technique for railroad-track stops with Count's deepened Georgia accent.

chapters.title: Greenville Meeting and the OEO Proposal
chapters.summary: At the Delta Ministry's December 1964 Greenville meeting, Geiger first articulates the community-health-center concept rooted in Pholela. On the grounded flight back to Boston, Count Gibson offers Tufts as sponsor. Geiger pitches Dr. William Kissick, then Elizabeth Shore, and in January 1965 secures from Sanford Kravitz at OEO research-and-demonstration funds; he returns with a $1.2 million two-site proposal for Columbia Point Boston and a Southern-rural site.

chapters.title: Selma 1965 and John Lewis
chapters.summary: Two days after Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, Geiger flies to Atlanta and rides with Dr. King's car back to Selma, where he listens to King, James Forman, and Hosea Williams debate whether to violate the injunction against the Montgomery march. Geiger is arrested with Dr. Richard Hausknecht by the Alabama State Medical Society; he diagnoses John Lewis's skull fracture and arranges with Count Gibson to transport Lewis and Ivanhoe Donaldson to Boston for treatment. Reverend James J. Reeb is killed days later.

chapters.title: Choosing Mound Bayou
chapters.summary: Geiger learns of the abandoned J.C. Campbell Junior College buildings in Mound Bayou and visits, meeting Father Guidry and discovering the all-Black town's autonomy, the 40-bed Taborian Hospital of the Knights and Daughters of Tabor, and the smaller Sarah Brown Hospital of the United Order of Friendship. Confronting R. Sargent Shriver Jr's stonewalling by occupying his office with the Tufts vice president, Geiger secures site approval from Dr. Julius B. Richmond. He then maneuvers the state medical society at the AMA Atlantic City meeting into endorsing Mound Bayou as the only acceptable Mississippi site.
related_events: OEO veto-proof routing of grants through Northern universities
Senator John C. Stennis attacks on Child Development Group of Mississippi

chapters.title: Building the Tufts-Delta Health Center
chapters.summary: Dr. John W. Hatch picks cotton in Bolivar County for three weeks during reconnaissance, then builds ten local health associations organized on the Black Baptist Church model. Recruitment brings Dr. Robert Smith, Dr. Aaron Shirley, Dr. Alvin F. Poussaint, Sister Mary Stella, Joe Disparti, Phyllis Cunningham, Dr. Helen Barnes, Dr. Harvey Sanders, Dr. Charles Humphrey, and clinical director David Weeks. Andrew B. James leads environmental work digging wells and sanitary privies. Clinical care begins in a rented church parsonage; daily volume rises from 15 to 100 patients in three days.
related_events: Senator Edward M. Kennedy 1966 visit to Columbia Point
creation of OEO office of health affairs

chapters.title: North Bolivar Farm Cooperative and Community Programs
chapters.summary: Asked to organize 100 vegetable gardens, thousands of families volunteer; recognizing displaced sharecroppers sitting on twelve-foot-deep topsoil, Dr. John W. Hatch and L.C. Dorsey build the North Bolivar County Farm Cooperative on twenty-acre donations into a 500-acre irrigated triple-crop farm involving four to six thousand people. Mrs. Robinson organizes the Round Lake meal program for isolated elderly. The health council runs bus transportation, WIC food, a library-bookstore cultural center, and an early-childhood intervention program.
related_events: collapse of the sharecropper system with mechanized agriculture

chapters.title: Legacy and Later Career
chapters.summary: Geiger details the federal community-health-center system's growth to over 1,200 centers serving 20 million people at 8,000-plus sites, with the 1975 statutory requirement that 51 percent of each center's board be current patients. He moves from Tufts to Stony Brook in 1971, then to the new CUNY Medical School (Sophie Davis School of Biomedical Education) in 1978 to train minority students for primary care in underserved areas. He cites the Medical-Legal Partnerships model and an Institute of Medicine honor.
keywords: Sophie Davis School of Biomedical Education

loc_video.caption: H. Jack Geiger oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in New York, New York, 2013 March 16
